constraints: User requires reconstructive React/CSS output, not image embeds.
User requires reusable/componentized design-system primitives, sections, whole pages, Storybook controls, and example parity.
Current Babysitter process for this run must remain agent-only with no shell task kinds.
Worktree already contains untracked process files; preserve them and avoid touching unrelated repo state.

The effective live execution path for this scope is centered on src/mockups plus shared code components and the example app, so changes should stay on those paths unless a directly imported shared primitive needs extension.
gaps: Seraph pages are structurally reconstructed but still materially off from the uploaded references in palette tuning, decorative density, frame geometry, and manuscript ornament placement.

SeraphPrimitives is still too coarse for exact reconstruction; several motifs are encoded as ad-hoc CSS classes instead of configurable reusable subcomponents for rails, medallions, botanical ornaments, manuscript side panels, and illuminated borders.

MockupPreviews keeps parity across Storybook and the example app, but the controls are mostly page-level. The Seraph-specific primitive stories do not yet expose the page motifs deeply enough for component-level reuse and configuration.

Dark mode exists at the gallery level and some surfaces, but the Seraph and wider mock surfaces still need systematic state adaptation and contrast review, especially for hover, active, disabled, chat-adjacent surfaces, and brass-on-dark combinations.

Code/diff surfaces are present and already used in chat/docs/Seraph pages, but adoption is incomplete and the code component family can still be broken into richer reusable subparts aligned with the user's request for configurable editor/code/diff building blocks.
The current CSS file is carrying a large amount of page-specific visual logic, which makes exact convergence harder than a more explicit primitive vocabulary would.
reusableSurfaces: SeraphWindow, SeraphSidebar, SeraphPromptBar, SeraphCard, SeraphTask, SeraphSummaryRow, SeraphComposer, SeraphAside, SeraphFolioBorder
runtimeCallPaths: Seraph mock pages depend on shared code surfaces from src/components/Code/Code.tsx through CodeBlock, CodeEditor, and DiffViewer

fixesApplied: Refactored the Seraph kit with explicit reusable HeroPlate and Divider primitives instead of keeping those opening/follow-through patterns implicit in page markup.
Updated both Seraph pages to consume the new kit primitives so the opening slab and divider rhythm are reusable across more use cases.
Expanded Storybook motif-level coverage with a dedicated kit-motifs story showing reusable hero, divider, and section-plate patterns outside the final page compositions.
Codified more framing and tone behavior in the shared CSS vocabulary, improving reuse across future Seraph surfaces.
residualRisks: Reference-level exactness is still not guaranteed; visual fidelity may still need another pass even though the reusable kit is materially stronger now.
Some darker and small-text states still need broader accessibility tightening across the full kit.
The ornament system is more explicit than before, but there is still room to extract even more motif primitives if the next review still rejects fidelity.
reusableSurfacesTouched: shared hero-seal / divider / hero-plate CSS vocabulary
page compositions migrated to shared kit primitives

a11yRisks: Small blueprint and parchment metadata still deserves another contrast-tightening pass.
Dark-mode adaptation is stronger than before but still not exhaustive across every newly introduced Seraph motif state.
issues: The Seraph kit is now materially more reusable, but the pages still do not yet reach near-exact reference matching. The remaining gap is less about missing primitives and more about the final visual calibration of palette, ornament density, and page-specific composition tension.
The refactor page still reads slightly too orderly compared with the reference. It needs a bit more irregular manuscript energy and stronger contrast between dominant and subordinate sections.
The bestiary page is much closer, but the ornament/frame relationship around the hero specimen can still be pushed further toward the reference feel.
Storybook motif coverage is improved, but some of the newly reusable visual language still lives more strongly in the pages than in isolated configurable stories.
nextEdits: Use the stronger kit to do one more calibration pass on the two pages: palette tuning, ornament balance, and section hierarchy tension.
Expose at least one more configurable Seraph motif story that isolates the hero/divider/frame language for reuse.
Tighten small-text contrast on the Seraph-specific metadata and blueprint-toned surfaces while preserving the manuscript look.
strengths: The kit now has explicit HeroPlate, Divider, SectionPlate, MarginNote, and richer card/task variants, which substantially improves reuse across future Seraph-like use cases.
The bestiary page now has a real focal artifact and much stronger manuscript hierarchy.
The run improved both visual fidelity and system reusability together instead of trading one off against the other.
The component/system-level accessibility fixes from earlier passes remain intact.

instructions: Converge the reconstructed Seraph mock pages to effectively exact fidelity against the uploaded references, while preserving reusable and configurable design-system primitives, Storybook parity with controls, example parity, dark-mode adaptation, accessible states, and shared code/diff/code-editor surfaces. Use a fresh Babysitter process and no shell kind tasks.
Use the previous review as the authoritative list of misses.
Fix the most visually important gaps first, then the architecture and a11y gaps that block reuse or dark-mode adaptation.
Favor reusable primitives, configurable sections, and shared CSS vocabulary, but do not hide behind abstraction if a direct redraw is still needed.
Keep the pages reconstructive, not image-based.
prompt.role: Senior React/CSS design-system engineer in a fidelity convergence loop
prompt.task: Apply the review feedback directly and improve the shared implementation without reducing scope.
